chunk_summary: This section discusses vegetarian protein anchors tailored to regional diets and common failure modes in protein intake, emphasizing practical solutions for children and adolescents to prevent nutritional deficiencies like iron deficiency anemia (IDA).
learning_objectives: Understand region-specific vegetarian protein anchors for balanced nutrition.
Identify common failure modes in protein intake among children and adolescents.
Learn practical interventions to prevent iron deficiency anemia in vegetarian households.
key_facts: Excess milk intake (>900–1000 mL/day) can displace iron-rich foods and increase IDA risk; limit milk to 500–600 mL/day for 1–5 year-olds.
Dal water is not a sufficient protein source; thick preparations like besan chilla or roasted chana are better alternatives.
Protein distribution is critical for adolescents, with one pulse/soy or dairy-based anchor recommended at breakfast and post-school/training.
Regional protein anchors include roti/paratha with dal in North India, idli/dosa with sambar in South India, and rice with fermented legumes in Eastern India.
Toddlers consuming only rice, potato, biscuits, and fruit are functionally on a low-protein diet despite vegetarian household practices.
reference_quote: Excess milk intake (often 900–1000 mL/day) can plateau weight gain and increase IDA risk by displacing iron-containing foods; limiting milk to about 500–600 mL/day in 1–5 year-olds is a high-yield intervention.
document_key_topics: Vegetarian protein anchors in regional diets
Common failure modes in protein intake
Nutritional interventions for children and adolescents
